title: Design a Vending Machine Leasing Management System

transcript.text: Design a Vending Machine Leasing Management System

blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the telemetry firehose and the automated billing — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: this is a fleet of physical machines in the field constantly reporting **real-time health and stock**, sitting on top of a **money-critical billing and contracts** system. So I have a high-volume, can-be-slightly-stale telemetry side glued to a low-volume, must-be-exact financial side — and the whole game is keeping those two worlds in the same system without letting the firehose threaten the money.

🏢 The problem says "leasing company across multiple cities" — that smells like **Amazon (operations + cost + work-backwards-from-the-customer)**, so I'll make cost and operability first-class and lead the deep dives with billing correctness and field-failure handling. Tell me if you'd rather I tilt Google-style (one deep distributed-systems problem) and I'll re-aim.

**Functional (the core flows — "above the line"):**

- **FR1 — Manage machine inventory & lease assignment.** Operators register vending machines, assign each to a business at a location, and attach it to a lease contract.
  - 🗣️ Plain words: "I can see every machine we own, where it physically is, which customer is renting it, and under what contract."
  - ⚡ Why it's hard: a machine moves through states (in-warehouse → deployed → under-repair → retired) and must never be double-leased to two businesses at once.

- **FR2 — Ingest real-time health & stock telemetry.** Each machine reports a heartbeat with stock counts per slot, temperature, door/coin-mech faults, and connectivity.
  - 🗣️ Plain words: "Every machine phones home about once a minute to say 'I'm alive, here's how full I am, here's anything broken.'"
  - ⚡ Why it's hard: ~200K machines on flaky cellular links produce a steady firehose of small writes, arriving late and out of order, and we must not drop a fault event.

- **FR3 — Monitor status & raise alerts.** A dashboard shows live machine health and stock; the system raises alerts for machine-down (missed heartbeats), low stock, and faults.
  - 🗣️ Plain words: "An ops person sees a map of red/green machines and gets pinged when one goes dark or runs low on Coke."
  - ⚡ Why it's hard: "machine-down" is the *absence* of an event — you can't react to a message that never arrived without a separate timeout mechanism.

- **FR4 — Automate billing from lease terms.** At each billing cycle, generate invoices from the contract (fixed monthly rent, per-machine fees, and any usage/revenue-share derived from telemetry).
  - 🗣️ Plain words: "On the 1st of the month the system bills each customer the right amount automatically — no spreadsheet."
  - ⚡ Why it's hard: money must be exact and idempotent — a retried billing run can never double-charge, and revenue-share needs trustworthy telemetry totals.

**Below the line (out of scope for today):** the physical payment hardware in the machine (card readers), consumer-facing purchase flow, route-planning for restockers, demand forecasting / ML reorder, tax filing, and the accounting-ledger general ledger integration (I'll emit events to it, not build it).

**Non-Functional:**

- **Telemetry availability & durability:** ingestion **99.99%**, never silently drop a fault/stock event.
  - 🗣️ Plain words: "The pipe that takes machine reports is basically always up, and once a machine's report is accepted we don't lose it."
  - 💥 What breaks without it: a fault event vanishes, a machine sits broken for days losing the customer money, and we can't honor an SLA or bill revenue-share correctly.

- **Read/dashboard latency:** machine status query **p99 < 300ms**; freshness **< 90s** end-to-end (event → visible).
  - 🗣️ Plain words: "When ops opens the dashboard it loads in under a third of a second and what they see is under a minute and a half old."
  - 💥 What breaks without it: ops chases stale red dots, dispatches a truck to a machine that already recovered, wastes a field visit (~$50–100 each).

- **Billing correctness:** **exactly-once**, strongly consistent, fully auditable; invoice math must reconcile to the penny.
  - 🗣️ Plain words: "Every customer is charged exactly once, the right amount, and we can prove how we got the number."
  - 💥 What breaks without it: a double-charge or a wrong invoice — direct revenue loss, chargebacks, and broken trust with B2B customers.

- **Availability (control plane: inventory, leases, billing):** **99.9%** (about 8.7 hours of downtime per year).
  - 🗣️ Plain words: "The contracts-and-money part is down less than nine hours a year."
  - 💥 What breaks without it: operators can't onboard machines or close deals; billing runs miss their window.

- **Scale:** horizontally scalable on machines (200K → millions), telemetry volume, and dashboard users.
  - 🗣️ Plain words: "We can add cities and machines without re-architecting."
  - 💥 What breaks without it: a single hot region or a big new contract tips the system over.

> ⚠️ Gaps I'd flag out loud: true *exactly-once* on the telemetry path is expensive; I'll make telemetry *at-least-once + idempotent* (so it's effectively once) and reserve real exactly-once accounting for the billing path where it actually matters. I'll be explicit about that split rather than pretend the firehose is transactional.

> 💬 "The trick is that this is two systems in a trenchcoat — a chatty telemetry pipeline that can be eventually consistent, and a small money system that must be exact — so I'll physically separate those paths and only join them at billing time."

🎙️ **Script:** "So we're building the back office for a vending-machine leasing business. There are four core flows: track the machines and which customer leases each one, swallow a constant stream of health-and-stock reports from the field, show that live on a dashboard with alerts, and then automatically bill each customer from their contract. The defining tension is that the telemetry side is high-volume and can be a little stale, while the billing side is low-volume but has to be exact to the penny. I'll keep those two paths separate and only join them when I compute a bill — and I'll be upfront that I'm making telemetry effectively-once rather than truly transactional."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

- **"Roughly how many machines, and how often does each one report?"**
  - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "That single number decides whether telemetry is a trivial trickle or a real firehose that needs a streaming pipeline."
  - ↔️ Design fork: If ~thousands of machines reporting every few minutes → a plain queue + Postgres is fine. If hundreds of thousands every minute → I need a partitioned log (Kafka) and a time-series store.
  - Assumption I'll proceed with: ~200K machines, heartbeat every ~60s — a real but modest firehose (~3.5K events/s).

- **"Is billing a fixed monthly rent, or does it include usage / revenue-share based on what the machine sold?"**
  - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "If it's pure fixed rent I never need telemetry for money; if it's revenue-share, my billing depends on trusting the telemetry totals, which is a much harder correctness problem."
  - ↔️ Design fork: If fixed → billing is a simple monthly batch off the contract. If usage-based → billing must aggregate trustworthy, deduplicated sales/stock telemetry per period.
  - Assumption I'll proceed with: a hybrid — fixed monthly lease fee **plus** an optional revenue-share line derived from telemetry. This forces the interesting join.

- **"How fresh must the dashboard be — live to the second, or is a minute fine?"**
  - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Sub-second live changes the whole serving tier (push/websockets, hot in-memory state); a minute lets me precompute and serve cheaply."
  - ↔️ Design fork: If seconds → push pipeline + in-memory live state. If ~minute → poll a precomputed status store.
  - Assumption I'll proceed with: < 90s freshness, poll-based — good enough for ops, far cheaper.

- **"What counts as 'machine down' and how fast must we detect it?"**
  - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Down is the absence of a heartbeat, so I need to decide the missed-beat threshold and that drives a separate timeout/sweeper mechanism."
  - ↔️ Design fork: If minutes matter (perishables, cold drinks) → tight 3-missed-beats timer + paging. If hours are fine → a periodic sweep job.
  - Assumption I'll proceed with: declare down after ~3 missed heartbeats (~3–5 min), alert ops.

- **"Are machines globally distributed or one country/region?"**
  - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Multi-region changes data residency, write topology, and latency for the field devices."
  - ↔️ Design fork: If one country → single-region, multi-AZ. If global → regional ingest with a central control plane.
  - Assumption I'll proceed with: multiple cities, single country/region for now, multi-AZ; design so regional ingest can be bolted on.

- **"Can a machine be reassigned between businesses, and is contract history auditable?"**
  - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Reassignment + audit means I can't just overwrite a machine's owner; I need an append-only lease history for billing disputes."
  - ↔️ Design fork: If YES → leases are immutable records with effective-dated ranges. If NO → a simple current-owner pointer.
  - Assumption I'll proceed with: yes — leases are effective-dated, immutable, and auditable.

- **"What's the consistency expectation for stock counts — exact or best-effort?"**
  - 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "If restockers act on stock, small staleness is fine; if we bill on units sold, it must reconcile."
  - ↔️ Design fork: If best-effort → eventual is fine for display. If billable → reconcile against a daily authoritative snapshot.
  - Assumption I'll proceed with: display is eventual; any billable usage is reconciled from a daily authoritative rollup.

🤝 Checkpoint: "So my working assumptions are ~200K machines reporting every minute, hybrid fixed-plus-revenue-share billing, ~90-second dashboard freshness, machine-down after ~3 missed beats, single region multi-AZ, and auditable effective-dated leases. Want to change any of those before I size it?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll only compute the few numbers that actually drive a decision and defer the rest.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Telemetry write rate (avg) | 200K machines ÷ 60s per heartbeat = 3,333 events/s | ~3.5K/s | "about three and a half thousand writes a second" | Needs a partitioned log + time-series store, not raw Postgres inserts |
| Telemetry write rate (peak) | 3,333/s × 2 (reconnect storms, restock bursts) = 6,667/s | ~7K/s | "up to seven thousand a second in a storm" | Sets Kafka partition count + ingest replica count |
| Telemetry events/day | 3,333/s × 86,400s = 288M | ~290M/day | "nearly 300 million little reports a day" | This is the LARGEST table — drives downsampling + TTL |
| Raw telemetry storage/day | 288M × 200 bytes/event = 57.6 GB | ~58 GB/day | "about 60 gigs a day raw" | Keep raw only ~30 days (~1.7 TB), roll up the rest |
| Raw telemetry/year if kept | 58 GB × 365 = 21 TB | ~21 TB/yr | "20-plus terabytes a year if I hoard it" | The flip point → downsample to hourly rollups |
| Dashboard read QPS | 5,000 users ÷ 30s poll = 167/s | ~170/s | "under two hundred reads a second" | Trivial — a read replica + cache handles it |
| Billing volume | 200K leases ÷ monthly = one batch run | ~200K invoices/mo | "a couple hundred thousand invoices, once a month" | Billing is a batch job, not a hot path |

> 🧠 **QPS** *(queries per second — how many requests hit a tier each second)*. **TTL** *(time-to-live — how long we keep a record before it auto-expires)*. **Time-series store** *(a database tuned for timestamped sensor data — fast appends, range scans by time, automatic downsampling)*.

**The one number that forces the design:** ingest is **~3.5K/s average, ~7K/s peak** — too much for naive Postgres row inserts (which top out around a few thousand small write txns/s before WAL and index maintenance choke), but trivial for a partitioned log. The flip threshold: if I tried to keep **raw per-minute telemetry forever**, that's ~21 TB/year and unqueryable — so I keep raw for ~30 days and **roll up to hourly per-machine aggregates**, which cuts the long-term footprint ~60x. The billing numbers are tiny by comparison, which confirms billing should be a separate batch path, not co-located with the firehose.

🎙️ **Script:** "The number that matters is telemetry: about three and a half thousand writes a second normally, up to seven thousand in a reconnect storm — that's nearly 300 million little events a day. That's too much to shove straight into a relational database, so it goes through a log into a time-series store, and I keep raw data only about a month before rolling it up to hourly summaries. Everything else — a couple hundred dashboard reads a second and a monthly billing run — is tiny, so I'll spend my budget on the ingest path and keep billing cleanly separate."

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first draft of the nouns — I'll add full field definitions in the data model.

- **Machine** — a physical vending unit we own: its hardware id, model, current state, and current location/assignment.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Think of it like a company laptop in an asset register — we always know which one it is, where it is, and who's got it."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you stop one machine being leased to two businesses?" → the Machine has exactly one *active* Lease at a time, enforced by a uniqueness constraint on (machine_id, active).

- **Business (Account)** — the customer entity that leases machines and receives invoices.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "The tenant on the lease — the company whose break room the machine sits in."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Can one business have machines in many cities under different terms?" → yes; a Business has many Leases, each with its own terms and location.

- **Lease (Contract)** — the agreement binding a Machine (or set of machines) to a Business: rent, fees, revenue-share %, start/end dates, billing cycle.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like a rental agreement — it says who rents what, for how much, for how long."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "A machine moves businesses mid-month — how do you bill?" → leases are effective-dated and immutable; billing prorates across the lease's active window.

- **Telemetry Reading** — one timestamped heartbeat from a machine: stock-per-slot, temperature, faults, connectivity.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like a fitness tracker ping — a tiny 'here are my vitals right now' message."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "What if two readings arrive out of order?" → readings are keyed by (machine_id, event_time) and we keep the latest by event-time, never wall-clock arrival.

- **Machine Status** — the materialized current view per machine (last-seen, up/down, stock summary, open faults) the dashboard reads.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "The single 'current state' card you see on the dashboard — derived from the raw pings."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Why a separate status entity?" → so the dashboard reads one precomputed row instead of scanning millions of raw readings.

- **Invoice** — the generated bill for a Business for a billing period: line items (rent, per-machine fees, revenue-share), total, status.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "The monthly bill — what they owe and why."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you avoid double-billing on a retry?" → each invoice has a natural idempotency key (business_id, period); a retry returns the existing invoice.

🎙️ **Script:** "The key nouns are Machines, the Businesses that lease them, and the Lease contracts that bind the two — those three are the money-and-inventory core. Then on the telemetry side there's the raw Reading and a derived Machine Status that the dashboard actually reads, and finally the Invoice. The one that's easy to underestimate is the Lease: because it's effective-dated and immutable, it's what lets me bill correctly when a machine changes hands mid-cycle. I'll firm up the exact fields in the data model."

## 5. API / Interface

Default REST over HTTPS. Telemetry ingest is a separate, high-throughput device-facing endpoint; everything else is the control-plane API. Caller identity always comes from a session/JWT or a device credential — never trusted from the body.

**FR1 — Register/assign a machine (control plane):**
- **POST /v1/machines** and **POST /v1/leases** — POST because each *creates new server state* (a new machine record, a new contract).
- Key request fields: `model: string` and `serialNumber: string` (the physical identity); `locationId: string` (where it's deployed). For the lease: `machineIds: string[]`, `businessId: string`, `terms: {monthlyRentCents, revenueSharePct, billingCycle}`, `startDate`. The client does **not** send `state` or `machineId` for a new machine — the server assigns the id and initializes state to `IN_WAREHOUSE`, so a client can't forge a machine into a billable state.

```
// Request
POST /v1/leases
{ "machineIds": ["mac_01H..."], "businessId": "biz_88", "terms": { "monthlyRentCents": 25000, "revenueSharePct": 10, "billingCycle": "MONTHLY" }, "startDate": "2026-07-01" }

// Response 201
{ "leaseId": "lease_42", "status": "ACTIVE", "machineIds": ["mac_01H..."], "createdAt": "2026-06-19T10:00:00Z" }
```
One key design decision: creating a lease **atomically flips each machine to a DEPLOYED, single-active-lease state** in the same transaction — the API rejects (409) if a machine already has an active lease, so we can never double-lease.

**FR2 — Telemetry ingest (device facing):**
- **POST /v1/telemetry** — POST appends a reading; authenticated by a per-device credential (mTLS or signed token), not a user JWT.
- Key request fields: `machineId`, `eventTime` (device clock), `stock: [{slot, count}]`, `faults: [...]`, `seq: int` (monotonic per-device sequence number — the **idempotency/natural key** so a re-sent reading dedups). The server stamps its own `ingestTime` and never trusts `eventTime` for billing windows beyond a sanity bound.

```
// Request
POST /v1/telemetry
{ "machineId": "mac_01H...", "seq": 90412, "eventTime": "2026-06-19T10:00:03Z",
  "stock": [{ "slot": "A1", "count": 3 }], "faults": [], "battery": 87 }

// Response 202
{ "accepted": true }
```
Key design decision: returns **202 Accepted**, not 200 — we durably append to the log and acknowledge; we do *not* synchronously process. `(machineId, seq)` makes retries idempotent.

**FR3 — Read machine status / list alerts (control plane):**
- **GET /v1/machines/{id}/status** and **GET /v1/businesses/{id}/machines?state=DOWN** — GET because it's a pure, cacheable read.
- Returns the materialized status: `{ machineId, lastSeen, up, stockSummary, openFaults, asOf }`. `asOf` is a freshness stamp so the UI can show how stale the view is.

**FR4 — Trigger/fetch billing (control plane, internal + admin):**
- **POST /v1/billing/runs** (internal scheduler) and **GET /v1/businesses/{id}/invoices** (admin/customer read).
- Key request field for the run: `period: "2026-06"` — used as part of the idempotency key `(businessId, period)` so re-running a billing job never produces a second invoice.

> 💬 "Notice telemetry returns 202 and is keyed by a per-device sequence number — accept fast, dedup later; and money endpoints are idempotent by (business, period) so retries are safe."

🎙️ **Script:** "On the control plane it's standard REST — POST to create machines and leases, GET to read status and invoices — and the important detail is that creating a lease atomically claims the machine so it can't be double-leased. The telemetry endpoint is different: it's device-authenticated, it returns 202 because we just durably append and process asynchronously, and every reading carries a per-device sequence number so a retransmit never counts twice. Billing is keyed by business-and-period, so a retried run is a no-op."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

I'll build one slice per above-the-line requirement, starting minimal and wiring each onto what already exists.

### 6.1 "Manage machine inventory & lease assignment"

This slice is the money-and-truth core: an operator creates machines and binds them to a business under a lease, and we must guarantee a machine has at most one active lease.

New components: a stateless **Control-Plane API**, a relational **Core DB (Postgres)** for machines/businesses/leases.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Control-Plane API | A stateless service handling machine/lease/business CRUD and auth | Needs transactional writes with business rules (single-active-lease); stateless = scale by adding replicas | Not a monolith with telemetry baked in (a firehose bug would take down billing); not serverless functions (long-lived DB connections + txns fit a service better) | One more service to deploy |
| Core DB (Postgres) | A relational database with rows, columns, and ACID transactions that all-or-nothing commit | Leases ↔ machines ↔ businesses are relational with hard invariants; a unique partial index enforces single-active-lease atomically | Not MongoDB (multi-row invariant + audit needs ACID; document model fights the relations); not DynamoDB (conditional cross-item txns are awkward and lock us in) | Manual sharding later if it grows; fine — control-plane data is small |

🎙️ NARRATION: "For inventory and leases I use a stateless control-plane service in front of Postgres. Postgres because this is relational, money-adjacent data with a hard rule — one active lease per machine — and a relational database lets me enforce that in a single transaction with a unique index. I considered Mongo and Dynamo, but the multi-row invariant and the need for an auditable, effective-dated lease history make a relational store the natural fit. The trade-off is I'll eventually shard manually, but this dataset is small so that's years away."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **ACID transaction** — an all-or-nothing database operation: either every step commits or none do. Here it lets us flip a machine to DEPLOYED and create its lease together, so we never end up half-assigned.
> **Unique partial index** — a database constraint that enforces uniqueness only on rows matching a condition (e.g. `active = true`). Here it guarantees at most one active lease per machine, enforced by the DB itself, not app code.
> **Stateless service** — a service that keeps no per-user state in memory, so any replica can serve any request. Here it lets us scale the API by just adding instances behind a load balancer.

**Machine assignment flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  OP[Operator console]
  OP -->|"1: POST lease"| API[Control-Plane API]
  subgraph Core["Control plane"]
    API -->|"2: begin txn"| PG[(Core DB Postgres)]
    PG -->|"3: check no active lease"| API
    API -->|"4: insert lease + flip machine"| PG
  end
  API -->|"5: emit LeaseCreated"| BUS[[Event bus]]
  classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
  classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
  classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
  classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
  class OP client
  class API svc
  class PG db
  class BUS queue
```

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. Operator POSTs a lease. The Control-Plane API owns this because it centralizes auth and the business invariants. State before: machine is IN_WAREHOUSE. What if the operator double-clicks? — the request is idempotent on a client-supplied request id.
2–4. Inside one Postgres transaction we check there's no existing active lease for any listed machine, insert the lease, and flip each machine to DEPLOYED. State change: machine now has exactly one active lease. What can go wrong: two operators race for the same machine → the unique partial index makes the second commit fail with a 409, not a double-lease.
5. We emit a `LeaseCreated` event so downstream (billing, dashboard) learns about it without a synchronous call. What if the bus is down? — we use a transactional outbox so the event is committed with the lease and published later, never lost.

> 💬 "The whole correctness of inventory hangs on one line: a unique index on (machine_id) where active — the database itself refuses to double-lease."

🎙️ **Script:** "An operator creates a lease through the control-plane API, and in a single Postgres transaction we verify the machine is free, create the contract, and mark the machine deployed. A unique partial index means the database itself guarantees one active lease per machine, so two operators racing can't both win. Then we publish a LeaseCreated event through a transactional outbox so billing and the dashboard find out reliably. The trade-off is an extra event hop, but it keeps the services decoupled."

### 6.2 "Ingest real-time health & stock telemetry"

This is the firehose: ~7K small writes/s at peak from flaky field devices. The goal is accept-fast-and-durable, then process asynchronously.

New components: a **Telemetry Gateway** (device-facing ingest), a **Kafka** log, a **Stream Processor**, a **Time-Series DB** for raw/rolled-up readings, and a **Status Store (Redis)** for the live per-machine view.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Telemetry Gateway | A thin device-auth ingest service that validates and appends readings to the log | Must absorb 7K/s, do mTLS device auth, and return 202 fast without touching the DB | Not direct-to-DB writes (Postgres chokes at a few K small txns/s); not a fat service that processes inline (couples latency to downstream) | Processing is async, so status lags slightly |
| Kafka | A durable, replayable log that buffers a firehose so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow | Decouples spiky ingest from steady processing; partition by machine_id keeps a machine's readings ordered on one partition | Not SQS (no ordered replay/retention for reprocessing); not Redis Streams (less durable at this retention) | Operational weight of running Kafka |
| Stream Processor | A consumer that dedups by (machine,seq), updates live status, and writes rollups | Centralizes idempotency + rollup logic; scales by partition | Not per-message lambdas (ordering + state per machine is awkward) | Another stateful component to checkpoint |
| Time-Series DB | A store tuned for timestamped data with fast appends and time-range scans + auto-downsampling | 290M events/day with time-range queries and TTL — exactly its sweet spot | Not Postgres (index bloat at this volume); not S3-only (no fast point/range query for recent data) | Eventual consistency for reads |
| Status Store (Redis) | An in-memory key-value store; here key=machine_id → current status blob | Dashboard needs sub-ms reads of "current state per machine" | Not query the TSDB each read (slower, hammers it at 170 QPS) | Volatile — rebuilt from TSDB/log on loss |

🎙️ NARRATION: "Telemetry comes into a thin gateway that authenticates the device, appends the reading to Kafka, and returns 202 immediately — it never touches the database on the hot path, so it can take seven thousand a second. Kafka buffers the firehose and, because I partition by machine id, every machine's readings stay ordered on one partition. A stream processor reads them, dedups on the per-device sequence number, updates a live status blob in Redis for the dashboard, and writes the reading into a time-series database that's built for this volume and auto-rolls-up old data. I picked Kafka over SQS because I want durable, replayable, ordered streams, and a TSDB over Postgres because 300 million rows a day would bloat a relational index."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Kafka** — a durable append-only log that holds events so slow consumers never lose data. Here it buffers the telemetry firehose and gives us ordered, replayable streams per machine.
> **Partition key** — the field Kafka uses to decide which partition an event lands on. We use machine_id so all of one machine's readings are ordered together.
> **Time-series database (TSDB)** — a database optimized for timestamped data: fast appends, time-range scans, and automatic downsampling/TTL. Here it stores raw readings (30 days) and hourly rollups (long term).
> **Idempotency (per-device seq)** — a monotonic counter the device stamps on each reading so a re-sent message is recognized and dropped. Here it makes at-least-once delivery effectively exactly-once.

**Telemetry ingest flow (hourglass)**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  M[Machine fleet]
  M -->|"1: POST telemetry"| GW[Telemetry Gateway]
  GW -->|"2: append"| K[[Kafka by machine_id]]
  subgraph Live["⚡ Live status path"]
    SP[Stream Processor]
    SP -->|"4: upsert status"| RD[(Status Store Redis)]
  end
  subgraph Durable["🔄 Durable / rollup path"]
    SP2[Stream Processor]
    SP2 -->|"5: write reading"| TS[(Time-Series DB)]
  end
  K -->|"3: consume"| SP
  K -->|"3b: consume"| SP2
```

↳ reuses existing: none new from §6.1; this is the telemetry plane.

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. Machine POSTs a reading over mTLS. The Gateway owns device auth so the rest of the system trusts the stream. What if the device's cell link drops mid-send? — it retries with the same `seq`, dedup handles it.
2. Gateway appends to Kafka and returns 202. State change: reading is durable in the log. What if Kafka is briefly unavailable? — the gateway buffers to a local disk-backed queue and the device also retries; we never ack what we didn't store.
3/3b. The stream processor(s) consume. Partitioning by machine_id means one machine's readings are processed in order. What if a consumer falls behind? — Kafka retains, so it catches up; freshness lags but nothing is lost.
4. Processor dedups by (machine_id, seq) and upserts the live status blob in Redis. State change: dashboard now reflects this reading. What if Redis is down? — status reads fall back to the latest reading in the TSDB (slower).
5. Processor writes the raw reading to the TSDB. State change: durable history for rollups/billing. What if the write fails? — it's retried from the Kafka offset; idempotent on (machine,seq).

> 💬 "Accept fast and durable at the gateway, then do all the real work asynchronously off Kafka — that's how a single modest cluster eats seven thousand writes a second."

🎙️ **Script:** "Devices post readings to a thin gateway that just authenticates them, appends to Kafka, and returns 202 — no database on the hot path. Kafka, partitioned by machine, buffers the firehose and keeps each machine's stream ordered. A stream processor then dedups on the device sequence number, updates a live Redis blob the dashboard reads, and writes the raw reading to a time-series database. The one decision that makes this work is moving processing off the request path — the device never waits on our storage, so a reconnect storm just fills the buffer instead of melting the system."

### 6.3 "Monitor status & raise alerts"

The dashboard reads live status cheaply, and we detect two alert classes: machine-down (absence of heartbeats) and low-stock/fault (presence of a bad reading).

New components: a **Read API** (serves status from Redis with a TSDB fallback), and a **Down-Detector / Sweeper** (a timer-based job that finds machines that stopped reporting) feeding an **Alerting service**.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Down-Detector / Sweeper | A periodic job scanning last-seen timestamps to flag machines silent past a threshold | "Down" is the absence of an event — only a timeout sweep, not a stream, can detect missing data | Not "wait for a down message" (a dead machine sends nothing); not per-machine timers in memory (200K timers is fragile) | Detection latency ≈ sweep interval (~1 min) |
| Alerting service | A service that dedups and routes alerts (page/email/webhook) with cooldowns | Need to avoid alert storms (one outage → thousands of pages) | Not raw per-event alerts (flapping machine spams ops) | Slight delay from dedup window |

🎙️ NARRATION: "The dashboard is a cheap read — it hits the Read API which serves the live status blob straight from Redis, falling back to the time-series DB if Redis is cold. The interesting part is detecting a machine that's *down*, because a dead machine sends nothing — there's no event to react to. So I run a sweeper every minute that scans last-seen timestamps and flags anything that's missed three beats, and that feeds an alerting service that dedups so one bad cell tower doesn't page ops a thousand times. Low-stock and faults are the easy case — they're present in a reading, so the stream processor raises them inline."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Sweeper / timeout job** — a periodic batch scan that finds records whose timestamp is older than a threshold. Here it detects machine-down by finding stale last-seen times — the only way to react to *missing* data.
> **Alert deduplication / cooldown** — suppressing repeat alerts for the same condition within a window. Here it stops a flapping machine or a regional outage from flooding ops with pages.

**Status read + down-detect flow**

```mermaid
  RAPI -->|"2: read"| RD[(Status Store Redis)]
  RAPI -.->|"2b: miss -> fallback"| TS[(Time-Series DB)]

  SW -->|"4: machine silent"| AL[Alerting service]
```

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. Dashboard GETs status. Read API owns serving so the device plane stays isolated. What if 5K users poll at once? — it's only ~170 QPS and Redis is sub-ms; a short cache TTL collapses duplicate polls.
2/2b. Read from Redis; on miss, fall back to the TSDB's latest reading. State change: none (pure read). What if both are stale? — `asOf` tells the UI exactly how old the data is.
3. Sweeper scans last-seen every ~60s. It owns down-detection because it's the only thing watching for absence. What if the sweeper itself dies? — it's a scheduled job with its own liveness alert (a dead-man's switch).
4–5. Silent machines (3 missed beats) become alerts; the alerting service dedups and pages ops. State change: an open alert is recorded. What if a whole city's cell network drops? — the alerting service detects the correlated burst and raises one "regional outage" alert instead of thousands.

> 💬 "The non-obvious bit: you can't get an event for a machine that died, so down-detection is a timeout sweep, not a stream subscription."

🎙️ **Script:** "Reads are easy — the dashboard hits the Read API, which serves the live status from Redis and falls back to the time-series DB. The subtle part is detecting a down machine: a dead machine sends nothing, so there's no event to catch. I run a sweeper every minute over last-seen timestamps, flag anything that's gone quiet, and route it through an alerting service that dedups so a regional outage becomes one alert, not ten thousand. Low-stock and faults are simpler because they arrive *in* a reading, so the stream processor raises those inline."

### 6.4 "Automate billing from lease terms"

Monthly, generate an exact, idempotent invoice per business: fixed rent + per-machine fees + revenue-share derived from a *reconciled* telemetry rollup. This is the money path — separate from the firehose.

New components: a **Billing Service** + a scheduled **Billing Run**, reading the **Core DB** (lease terms) and a **daily authoritative usage rollup** from the TSDB; it writes invoices to the Core DB and emits events to an external **Payments/Ledger** system.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Billing Service | A service that turns lease terms + usage into invoices, idempotently | Money must be exact; it reads authoritative inputs and writes once per (business, period) | Not inline-at-event billing (telemetry is eventual and noisy — can't bill on a raw stream) | Bills on a cycle, not real-time (correct, not instant) |
| Usage Rollup (daily) | A nightly job that aggregates raw telemetry into an authoritative per-machine per-day units-sold/uptime figure | Revenue-share needs a trustworthy, deduplicated number, not live counts | Not live stream counts (out-of-order/late events make them non-final) | A day of latency before usage is billable |
| Payments/Ledger (external) | The third-party system that actually charges and records money | Don't build payments; emit events and reconcile | Not an in-house ledger (huge scope, compliance) | Dependency on an external system |

🎙️ NARRATION: "Billing runs on a schedule, not on the live stream, because telemetry is eventual and arrives late and out of order — I can't bill money off noisy real-time counts. So a nightly rollup turns raw readings into an authoritative per-machine, per-day usage number, and at the billing cycle the billing service combines that with the lease terms to produce one invoice per business per period. It's idempotent on business-and-period, so a retried run never double-bills. I emit the charge to an external payments and ledger system rather than building one. The trade-off is usage is a day stale at billing time, which is completely fine for a monthly invoice."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Idempotency key (business, period)** — a natural key that uniquely identifies one invoice. Here a re-run of the billing job finds the existing invoice instead of creating a second, preventing double-charges.
> **Authoritative rollup** — a finalized, deduplicated aggregate computed after late data has settled. Here it's the trustworthy usage number revenue-share is billed on, vs. the live (non-final) stream counts.
> **Transactional outbox** — writing an event into the same DB transaction as the data change, then publishing it separately, so the event is never lost or sent without the data. Here it links invoice creation to the payments event reliably.

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. Scheduler starts a run for a period. The Billing Service owns money logic centrally. What if it fires twice? — the run is idempotent on (business, period).
2–3. Read lease terms from Postgres and the finalized usage rollup from the TSDB. State change: none yet. What if a lease changed mid-month? — effective-dated leases let us prorate exactly.
4. Upsert the invoice keyed by (business, period) in one transaction. State change: an invoice exists exactly once. What if the run crashes after some businesses? — re-running skips those already invoiced (idempotent) and finishes the rest.
5–6. Emit `InvoiceIssued` via the outbox; the external payments system charges. What if the charge fails? — the invoice is marked PENDING/FAILED and retried with the same idempotency key; no double-charge.

> 💬 "Bill on a finalized daily rollup, not the live stream, and key invoices by business-and-period — that's how money stays exact even though telemetry is eventual."

🎙️ **Script:** "Billing is deliberately separate from the firehose. A nightly rollup finalizes usage into a trustworthy per-machine number, and once a cycle the billing service combines that with the lease terms to issue one invoice per business per period. Everything is keyed by business-and-period so a retried run can't double-bill, leases are effective-dated so a mid-month move prorates correctly, and the actual charge goes out to an external payments system through a transactional outbox. The trade-off is usage is a day old at billing time — totally fine for a monthly bill, and it buys me exactness."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — ENTITY TABLE**

| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency guarantee |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Machine | machine_id (PK), serial, model, state, location_id, active_lease_id | Postgres | machine_id | Strong |
| Business | business_id (PK), name, billing_contact, account_status | Postgres | business_id | Strong |
| Lease | lease_id (PK), machine_id, business_id, active (bool), rent_cents, rev_share_pct, start_date, end_date | Postgres | business_id | Strong (unique partial index on machine_id WHERE active) |
| Telemetry Reading | (machine_id, event_time) PK, seq, stock[], faults[], temp | Time-Series DB | machine_id | Eventual; idempotent by (machine_id, seq) |
| Machine Status | machine_id (PK) → last_seen, up, stock_summary, open_faults | Redis | machine_id | Eventual (≤90s) |
| Usage Rollup | (machine_id, day) PK, units_sold, uptime_pct | Time-Series DB | machine_id | Finalized daily (authoritative) |
| Invoice | (business_id, period) unique, lease_lines[], total_cents, status | Postgres | business_id | Strong, exactly-once |

**PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS**

**🗄️ PostgreSQL — used for: Machine, Business, Lease, Invoice**
1. What it is (plain words): a relational database that stores data in rows and columns with strict rules, and guarantees a multi-step operation either fully commits or fully rolls back.
2. Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice: we need an atomic "verify-free-then-assign" on machines (single active lease) and exactly-once invoice writes — only a relational DB with row-level locking and unique constraints can enforce those invariants in one transaction.
3. What we considered instead and why we rejected each:
   - **MongoDB** — flexible schema is nice, but the single-active-lease and invoice-uniqueness invariants need multi-row ACID; Mongo's transactions are slower and less natural for these relations.
   - **DynamoDB** — great for KV, but the conditional cross-entity transaction for lease assignment is awkward and it's vendor lock-in for data that's small anyway.
   - **MySQL** — would work identically; I default to Postgres for richer constraints (partial indexes), JSON support, and it's the modern standard.
4. Trade-off we accept: manual sharding if it ever grows huge — but control-plane data is small (hundreds of MB), so this is years away.
5. 🗣️ How to say it out loud: "Machines, leases, and invoices go in Postgres because this is money and inventory with hard rules — one active lease per machine, one invoice per period. I looked at Dynamo and Mongo, but the atomic assign-if-free and the invoice uniqueness are exactly what a relational DB does best, and the dataset is small so I'm not worried about scale here."

**🗄️ Time-Series DB (e.g. TimescaleDB / InfluxDB) — used for: Telemetry Reading, Usage Rollup**
1. What it is (plain words): a database built for timestamped sensor data — extremely fast appends, time-range scans, and automatic downsampling and expiry of old data.
2. Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice: 290M appends/day with queries like "this machine's readings over the last hour" and automatic rollup-and-TTL — that's precisely what a TSDB is engineered for.
3. What we considered instead and why we rejected each:
   - **Postgres** — index maintenance and table bloat at 290M rows/day would degrade everything; the firehose would threaten the money tables if co-located.
   - **Cassandra** — would handle the write volume, but we'd hand-build the rollups and TTL a TSDB gives for free.
   - **S3 only** — cheap and durable, but no fast point/range query for the recent-data dashboards.
4. Trade-off we accept: eventual consistency and an extra store to operate — fine, because this data is display + reconcilable, not the source of truth for money.
5. 🗣️ How to say it out loud: "Raw telemetry goes in a time-series database because it's built for hundreds of millions of timestamped appends a day with automatic rollups and expiry. I keep raw for a month and roll up to hourly, which cuts long-term storage about sixty-fold. I deliberately keep it off Postgres so the firehose can never threaten the billing tables."

**🗄️ Redis — used for: Machine Status (live dashboard view)**
1. What it is (plain words): an in-memory key-value store that returns data in well under a millisecond.
2. Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice: the dashboard needs the current state of any machine instantly; a key=machine_id lookup in memory is the cheapest possible read.
3. What we considered instead and why we rejected each:
   - **Query the TSDB each read** — slower and pounds the TSDB at 170 QPS for data that barely changes.
   - **In-process cache** — doesn't share across API replicas; Redis is the shared truth.
4. Trade-off we accept: it's volatile, so on loss we rebuild it from the TSDB / Kafka replay — acceptable because it's a derived view.
5. 🗣️ How to say it out loud: "The live status the dashboard reads sits in Redis, keyed by machine id, so a read is a sub-millisecond memory lookup. It's just a cache of the latest reading — if Redis dies I rebuild it from the time-series store or replay Kafka."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency summary**

| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Create/assign lease | Postgres | Strong (serializable on the machine row) | Must never double-lease; invariant enforced by unique index |
| Issue invoice | Postgres | Strong, exactly-once | Money; (business, period) uniqueness prevents double-bill |
| Write telemetry reading | TSDB | Eventual, idempotent | High volume; dedup by (machine, seq) makes it effectively once |
| Read machine status (dashboard) | Redis | Eventual (≤90s) | Display tolerates seconds of staleness; `asOf` exposes it |
| Read-your-own-writes (operator just assigned a machine) | Postgres | Strong (read from primary) | Operator must immediately see their own change; read primary after write |

🎙️ **Script:** "The core — machines, leases, invoices — lives in Postgres because it's money and inventory with hard invariants, and I lean on a unique partial index for single-active-lease and a unique (business, period) for one-invoice-per-cycle. The telemetry firehose goes to a time-series database, deliberately separate so it can't threaten the money tables, with raw kept a month and rolled up after. A Redis cache holds the live status the dashboard reads. I did consider putting everything in one Postgres, but 300 million telemetry rows a day would bloat it and risk the billing path, so splitting the stores is the senior call here."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 If you get stuck: come back to "two planes — eventual telemetry, exact money — joined only at billing," and reason forward from there.

### How do we ingest the telemetry firehose without dropping fault events?

**#### Bad: write each reading straight to Postgres**
🗣️ What this is in plain words: every time a machine phones home, we immediately insert a row in the main database.
Approach: the device POSTs, the API does an `INSERT` into a readings table in Postgres, returns 200.
Why people try this: it's the simplest thing that works at small scale and keeps everything in one DB.
⚠️ What breaks: at ~7K small inserts/s, Postgres WAL flushing and index maintenance choke — a single primary tops out around a few thousand small write txns/s. The connection pool (the fixed set of open DB connections, typically 50–200) saturates, latency spikes, and — fatally — the telemetry load now competes with billing and lease writes on the same DB, so the firehose can take down the money path.
🔁 What forces the upgrade: the moment write rate exceeds a few thousand/s, or the moment you realize telemetry and billing must not share fate.

**#### Good: buffer through a queue, async insert**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: Bad coupled a high-volume write to a synchronous DB insert on the request path; Good decouples them with a durable buffer so the device never waits on storage.
🗣️ What this is in plain words: the machine drops its reading in a mailbox and leaves; a worker processes the mailbox at its own pace.
Approach: gateway appends to **Kafka** (a durable, replayable log), returns 202; a consumer batches inserts into a store. Devices retry on failure.
Why people try this: it absorbs spikes and protects the DB.
⚠️ What breaks: if the consumer just batch-inserts into Postgres, you've only delayed the storage problem; and naive at-least-once delivery double-counts readings on retry, corrupting any usage-based billing.
🔁 What forces the upgrade: out-of-order/late events and retries mean you need idempotency + a store that actually likes this volume.

**#### Great: Kafka + idempotent stream processing into a TSDB**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: Good still wrote to the wrong store and didn't dedup; Great fixes both — a time-series store sized for the volume, and dedup on a per-device sequence number.
🗣️ What this is in plain words: readings flow through an ordered log, a processor throws away duplicates by their sequence number, updates a live status cache, and files the reading in a database built for sensor data.
Approach: partition Kafka by machine_id (so each machine's readings are ordered on one partition); the stream processor dedups on (machine_id, seq), upserts Redis status, and writes to the TSDB. Late events land by event-time; a daily rollup finalizes usage after lateness settles.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  D[Reading seq=n] --> K[[Kafka part=machine_id]]
  K --> P[Processor dedup by machine+seq]
  P -->|live| RD[(Redis status)]
  P -->|durable| TS[(Time-Series DB)]
  classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
  classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
  classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
  class P svc
  class K queue
  class RD,TS db
```

🔢 Decision-forcing math: 7K/s peak ÷ a Postgres ceiling of ~3K small write-txns/s = 2.3x over budget → must offload; one Kafka partition handles ~10MB/s, our 7K × 200B ≈ 1.4MB/s fits in a handful of partitions with headroom.
✅ Failure matrix:
| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Device retransmits a reading | Same (machine, seq) → processor drops the duplicate |
| Consumer crashes mid-batch | Resumes from last committed Kafka offset; idempotent writes → no dupes |
| Reading arrives 5 min late | Filed by event_time into the right bucket; daily rollup includes it |
| Kafka broker dies | Replication factor 3 → another broker has it; no loss |
| Redis status lost | Rebuilt from latest TSDB readings / Kafka replay |

🎙️ **Script:** "I take readings through Kafka partitioned by machine, so each machine's stream is ordered, and a processor dedups on the device's own sequence number before writing — that turns at-least-once delivery into effectively exactly-once. The live status goes to Redis for the dashboard and the durable reading to a time-series database built for this volume. The key insight is never writing the firehose to Postgres, both because the volume would choke it and because I refuse to let telemetry share fate with billing."
🧠 If they ask "why not exactly-once delivery from Kafka?": "True end-to-end exactly-once needs transactional sinks and adds latency. Idempotent writes keyed by (machine, seq) give the same result far more simply, so I take effectively-once over the machinery of exactly-once."

### How do we bill exactly-once, including usage-based revenue-share?

**#### Bad: bill off the live telemetry stream**
🗣️ What this is in plain words: as readings come in, keep a running tally and charge from that.
Approach: increment a per-machine revenue counter on each reading; at month end, bill the counter.
Why people try this: it feels real-time and avoids a separate batch.
⚠️ What breaks: telemetry is eventual, out-of-order, and occasionally duplicated — the live counter is never *final*, so you bill a number that keeps changing. A late reading after invoicing means you under- or over-charged. This is a correctness catastrophe for money.
🔁 What forces the upgrade: you cannot bill on a non-final number — you need a settled, deduplicated input.

**#### Great: idempotent billing off a finalized daily rollup**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: Bad billed a moving target; Great bills a finalized, deduplicated rollup and makes the invoice itself idempotent.
🗣️ What this is in plain words: each night we lock in an official "units sold today" per machine after late data settles; once a month we add those up with the contract and produce one bill.
Approach: a nightly job rolls raw telemetry into an authoritative `(machine, day)` usage figure (dedup by seq, include late events past the watermark). The billing run reads lease terms (effective-dated, so mid-month moves prorate) plus the finalized rollup, and upserts an invoice keyed by `(business, period)`. The charge is emitted via a transactional outbox to the external payments system, retried with the same idempotency key.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  R[Raw telemetry] --> RU[Nightly rollup finalize units/day]
  RU --> TS[(Usage rollup)]
  L[(Lease terms effective-dated)] --> B[Billing Service]
  TS --> B
  B -->|upsert by business+period| INV[(Invoice)]
  B --> PAY[Payments external idempotent]
  classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
  classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
  classDef ext fill:#3a2a4d,stroke:#6B46C1,color:#e6edf3
  class RU,B svc
  class TS,INV,L db
  class PAY ext
```

🔢 Decision-forcing math: 200K invoices in one nightly window ÷ even a modest 200 invoices/s = ~17 minutes — trivially within a billing window, so batch is clearly the right shape, not real-time.
✅ Failure matrix:
| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Billing run retried | (business, period) upsert → existing invoice returned, no double-bill |
| Crash mid-run | Re-run skips already-issued invoices, finishes the rest |
| Late telemetry after rollup | Caught by next-day rollup; a correction line on next invoice |
| Payment charge fails | Invoice marked FAILED, retried with same idempotency key — no double-charge |
| Lease changed mid-month | Effective-dated terms → prorated automatically |

🎙️ **Script:** "I never bill off the live stream because it's eventual and never final. Instead a nightly job finalizes a deduplicated units-sold figure per machine, and once a month the billing service combines that with the effective-dated lease terms to produce exactly one invoice per business per period — keyed by business-and-period so a retry can't double-bill. The actual charge goes to an external payments system through an outbox with an idempotency key. The principle is: money is computed from settled, authoritative inputs, never from a moving target."
🧠 If they ask "what about a reading that arrives after you've billed?": "It's rare past the watermark, but I handle it with a correction line on the next invoice rather than reopening a closed one — closed periods stay immutable for audit."

### How do we detect a machine that goes silent (machine-down)?

**#### Good: timeout sweeper over last-seen**
🗣️ What this is in plain words: since a dead machine can't tell us it's dead, we periodically check who hasn't checked in.
Approach: every ~60s a sweeper scans the Redis status (or an index of last_seen) and flags machines that missed ~3 beats; flagged machines raise an alert.
Why people try this... well, it's the only thing that works — you cannot subscribe to an event that never fires.
⚠️ What breaks: a naive sweep raises per-machine alerts, so a regional cell outage pages ops thousands of times (alert storm); and a single global sweeper is a single point of failure.

**#### Great: sharded sweep + correlated alert dedup**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: Good's single sweeper didn't scale or dedup; Great shards the sweep by machine range and adds correlation so one outage is one alert.
🗣️ What this is in plain words: several workers each watch a slice of the fleet, and the alerting layer groups related failures into a single incident.
Approach: partition the sweep by machine_id range across workers (and run them as scheduled jobs with a dead-man's-switch on the sweeper itself). The alerting service dedups by (machine, condition) with a cooldown and detects correlated bursts (many machines in one city/region down at once → one "regional outage" alert).
🔢 Decision-forcing math: scanning 200K last-seen entries every 60s is ~3.3K checks/s — trivial; the cost is alert fan-out, which dedup bounds to O(incidents), not O(machines).
🎙️ **Script:** "A down machine sends nothing, so detection has to be a timeout sweep, not a subscription. I run sharded sweepers over last-seen timestamps every minute and flag anything that's missed three beats. The real engineering is in the alerting: I dedup by machine-and-condition and correlate bursts, so a whole-city cell outage becomes one incident instead of thousands of pages. And the sweeper has its own liveness alarm so a dead detector doesn't fail silently."
🧠 If they ask "why not push notifications from machines on shutdown?": "A clean shutdown can send a last-gasp message, but most failures — power loss, cell drop, crash — send nothing, so I can't rely on it; the timeout sweep is the dependable backstop."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean)**
- **Telemetry ingest path: 99.99%** = under ~52 minutes of downtime per year. Achieved by a stateless gateway behind a load balancer across **multi-AZ** (multiple data centres in one region, so if one building loses power another takes over automatically), Kafka with replication factor 3, and device-side retry/buffering.
- **Control + money path: 99.9%** = about 8.7 hours/year. Achieved by multi-AZ Postgres with a synchronous standby and automatic failover.
- **Dashboard read path: 99.9%**, with graceful degradation (stale-but-up) rather than hard failure.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table**

| Component | What breaks if it's down | How the system degrades gracefully (what the user sees) | Recovery action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Telemetry Gateway | New readings can't be accepted | Devices buffer locally and retry; once back, backlog drains — ops sees a brief freshness lag, no data loss | Auto-scale replicas; LB health-check reroute |
| Kafka | Stream processing stalls | Gateway buffers to disk; dashboard shows last-known status with an older `asOf` | Broker replication + auto-recovery; replay |
| Stream Processor | Status stops updating | Dashboard freshness lags; readings safe in Kafka | Restart from last offset; idempotent catch-up |
| Redis (status) | Live reads miss | Read API falls back to TSDB latest reading (slower, ~50ms) — users still see status | Rebuild cache from TSDB / Kafka replay |
| Time-Series DB | New rollups/history reads fail | Live status (Redis) still served; billing rollup retried when back | Multi-node TSDB; restore from snapshot |
| Postgres (core) | No lease/invoice writes; reads via replica | Operators can read but not create leases; billing run defers | Sync standby failover (RPO≈0) |
| Payments (external) | Charges can't be sent | Invoices created and marked PENDING; retried | Idempotent retry with backoff + circuit breaker |

Circuit breakers, retries, backoff: every external/inter-service call uses exponential backoff with jitter and a circuit breaker (after N failures, stop calling for a cooldown and fail fast) so a slow payments API doesn't cascade into thread exhaustion.

**9C — RPO / RTO per data class (plain definitions first)**
RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if a crash happens right now; RPO≈0 means we lose nothing because every write is synchronously replicated before we acknowledge it. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long until the service works again after a crash.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Leases & invoices (money) | ≈0 | < 5 min | Postgres synchronous standby + automatic failover |
| Raw telemetry | ≤ seconds | < 10 min | Kafka RF3 (durable log is source of truth); replay |
| Usage rollups | rebuildable | minutes | Recompute from raw telemetry |
| Machine status (Redis) | disposable | seconds | Rebuild from TSDB / Kafka |

Disaster recovery: daily encrypted Postgres snapshots + continuous WAL archiving (point-in-time restore); Kafka topics mirrored; a documented, periodically *tested* restore into a staging cluster. Multi-AZ by default; a multi-region active-passive plan with cross-region replication is the next step if uptime targets tighten, with failover triggered by a region health-check breaching for > 2 minutes.

**9D — Cost breakdown**
Top 3 drivers: (1) **Streaming + processing compute** — the Kafka cluster and stream processors run 24/7 to eat the firehose; this is the biggest line, not storage. (2) **Time-series storage + IOPS** — 290M events/day; the optimization that cuts the most is aggressive downsampling (raw 30d → hourly rollups), shrinking long-term footprint ~60x. (3) **Cellular connectivity / data egress** for 200K devices (often billed per-SIM) — reducing heartbeat frequency or delta-encoding readings trims it. Rough order of magnitude: low **tens of thousands of dollars/month**, dominated by streaming compute. The single biggest cost lever is telemetry retention/granularity.

🎙️ **Script:** "When something fails, the user mostly sees *staleness, not an outage*: if Redis dies the dashboard reads a bit slower from the time-series store; if Kafka or the processor stalls, machines keep buffering and the dashboard just shows an older as-of time; if payments is down, invoices sit pending and retry. The money path is the strict one — synchronous standby gives near-zero data loss and sub-five-minute failover. And because the raw log is the source of truth, recovery almost everywhere is just replay. Cost is dominated by the always-on streaming compute, and my biggest lever is how long and how finely I keep telemetry."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Separate telemetry plane (TSDB) vs one unified Postgres**
1. What we chose and why: split stores so the 290M-events/day firehose can never starve or take down the money tables.
2. What we gave up: a single source of truth and simple cross-store joins; we now reconcile telemetry into billing via a rollup.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "It's like keeping the noisy factory floor in a different building from the accounting office."
4. When this reverses: if machine count drops to a few thousand reporting infrequently, one Postgres handles it all and the extra plane isn't worth the ops cost.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "I split telemetry into a time-series store so the firehose can't threaten billing — if volume were small I'd just keep one database."

**🔀 Decision: Eventual consistency for status vs strong/real-time**
1. What we chose and why: status is eventual (≤90s) so reads are a cheap Redis lookup serving thousands of users.
2. What we gave up: to-the-second liveness on the dashboard.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "A slightly old but instant map beats a perfectly live but slow one for ops work."
4. When this reverses: if a use case needs second-level reaction (e.g. cold-chain spoilage alarms), I add a push/websocket live path for that signal.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "The dashboard's a minute fresh on purpose — it makes reads trivially cheap; I'd only add live push for something time-critical like temperature."

**🔀 Decision: Batch billing off a finalized rollup vs real-time stream billing**
1. What we chose and why: bill from a settled, deduplicated daily rollup so money is exact and auditable.
2. What we gave up: real-time revenue visibility in the invoice itself.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "We close the books for the day before we trust the number, like any accountant."
4. When this reverses: if billing became pure fixed rent with no usage component, I'd drop the rollup dependency entirely and bill straight from the contract.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "I bill on a finalized nightly rollup, not the live stream, because you can't charge money on a number that's still changing."

**🔀 Decision: At-least-once + idempotent telemetry vs true exactly-once**
1. What we chose and why: idempotency by (machine, seq) gives effectively-once with far less machinery than transactional exactly-once.
2. What we gave up: the theoretical cleanliness (and latency cost) of end-to-end exactly-once delivery.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "We let duplicates happen and just ignore the repeats, instead of building expensive plumbing to prevent them."
4. When this reverses: if a downstream consumer couldn't be made idempotent, I'd pay for exactly-once delivery there.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Telemetry is at-least-once but deduped by a per-device sequence number, so it's exactly-once in effect without the overhead."

🎙️ **Script:** "The calls I'd flag: I split telemetry into its own store so the firehose can't threaten the money — I'd unify only at small scale. I made status eventual to keep reads cheap, adding live push only for something time-critical. I bill from a finalized nightly rollup, never the live stream, because money can't ride a moving number. And telemetry is at-least-once but idempotent, which is exactly-once in practice. Every one of these trades a little precision or simplicity for scale and correctness where each matters most."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two operators try to lease the same machine at the same moment — who wins?"**
The mechanism (what actually happens): both transactions try to insert an active lease for that machine; a **unique partial index** on `(machine_id) WHERE active = true` lets exactly one commit and the other fails with a constraint violation, which the API returns as a 409 Conflict.
🗣️ In plain words: the database itself only allows one active lease per machine, so the second person just gets told "already taken."
💬 One-liner: "The unique index makes double-leasing impossible at the database level — one commits, the other 409s."

**❓ "How do you detect a machine that just died and sends nothing?"**
The mechanism: a sweeper job scans last-seen timestamps every ~60s and flags machines that missed ~3 heartbeats; the alerting service dedups and correlates so one outage isn't thousands of pages.
🗣️ In plain words: since a dead machine can't tell us it's dead, we periodically check who hasn't phoned home lately.
💬 One-liner: "Down is the absence of an event, so it's a timeout sweep over last-seen, not a subscription."

**❓ "It's a reconnect storm — 7,000 readings a second hit at once. What happens?"**
The mechanism: the gateway appends to Kafka and returns 202 without touching a database; Kafka (a durable log) absorbs the spike and the processor drains it at its own pace, so freshness lags briefly but nothing drops.
🗣️ In plain words: the flood goes into a buffer that the system empties steadily, instead of all hitting the database at once.
💬 One-liner: "Kafka absorbs the burst; the only symptom is a few seconds of extra dashboard lag."

**❓ "Can the same machine reading be counted twice in billing?"**
The mechanism: each reading carries a per-device monotonic `seq`; the processor dedups on `(machine_id, seq)`, and billing reads a finalized daily rollup, so duplicates are eliminated before money is computed.
🗣️ In plain words: every reading has a serial number, and we ignore repeats before we ever add up a bill.
💬 One-liner: "Dedup by per-device sequence number means a retransmit never inflates a bill."

**❓ "Can a customer be billed twice for the same month?"**
The mechanism: invoices are keyed unique on `(business_id, period)`; a re-run of the billing job upserts and returns the existing invoice rather than creating a second.
🗣️ In plain words: there's room for only one invoice per customer per month, so re-running the job changes nothing.
💬 One-liner: "Invoices are idempotent on business-and-period — a retried run is a no-op."

**❓ "What if the payment processor is down during a billing run?"**
The mechanism: invoices are still created and marked PENDING; the charge event sits in the outbox and is retried with exponential backoff behind a circuit breaker, using the same idempotency key so no double-charge.
🗣️ In plain words: we still write the bill, we just keep politely retrying the actual charge until it goes through.
💬 One-liner: "Invoices are created regardless; the charge retries idempotently when payments recovers."

**❓ "How do you stop a client from forging a machine into a billable state or faking telemetry?"**
The mechanism: control-plane callers are identified by JWT and can only act within their org; new machines are server-initialized to IN_WAREHOUSE (clients can't set state). Telemetry is device-authenticated via mTLS/signed credentials, and billing trusts only the server-side reconciled rollup, not raw device-claimed totals.
🗣️ In plain words: the client never gets to declare "this machine is earning money" — the server controls state, and devices prove who they are.
💬 One-liner: "State and money are server-set; devices authenticate with mTLS and we bill on reconciled data, not device claims."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part of this system, and how would you cut it?"**
The mechanism: the always-on streaming compute (Kafka + processors) and time-series IOPS dominate — not the control-plane DB. The biggest lever is downsampling raw telemetry (30-day raw → hourly rollups, ~60x reduction) and reducing heartbeat frequency / delta-encoding readings.
🗣️ In plain words: the constant firehose costs the most, so we keep less of it for less time and send smaller messages.
💬 One-liner: "It's the 24/7 streaming and telemetry storage; aggressive downsampling cuts it the most."

**❓ "A machine moves from business A to business B mid-month — how is billing correct?"**
The mechanism: leases are effective-dated and immutable; the billing run prorates each lease across its active days in the period, so A is billed for its window and B for theirs.
🗣️ In plain words: because each contract records exactly when it started and ended, we split the month between the two customers automatically.
💬 One-liner: "Effective-dated leases let billing prorate the month across both businesses precisely."

**❓ "How do you guarantee an operator sees a machine they just assigned (read-your-own-writes)?"**
The mechanism: the assignment commits to the Postgres primary; the operator's immediate follow-up read is routed to the primary (not a lagging replica) within the session, so they always see their own write.
🗣️ In plain words: right after you make a change, we read from the master copy so you never see stale data.
💬 One-liner: "Read-from-primary after a write guarantees the operator sees their own assignment instantly."

**❓ "How would you add a new metric, like predicted-stockout-time, later?"**
The mechanism: telemetry already flows through Kafka, so I add a new consumer that computes the metric and writes a new rollup/status field — ingestion, partitioning, and serving are unchanged.
🗣️ In plain words: the data's already flowing, so a new feature is just a new reader of the same stream.
💬 One-liner: "New metric = new Kafka consumer; the pipeline doesn't change."

**❓ "How would you support a 10x bigger fleet — 2 million machines?"**
The mechanism: telemetry scales by adding Kafka partitions and processor instances (it's partition-parallel by machine_id); the TSDB scales horizontally; the control plane is tiny so it's unaffected. The only watch item is hot partitions, handled by sub-partitioning very chatty machines.
🗣️ In plain words: we just add more lanes to the telemetry highway; the contracts side barely notices.
💬 One-liner: "Add Kafka partitions and processors — the firehose is embarrassingly parallel by machine."

**❓ "Is there any PII, and how do you handle retention/erasure?"**
The mechanism: telemetry holds no consumer PII (it's machine vitals); business contact info in Postgres is the sensitive data, under a retention policy with deletes keyed by business_id. Closed invoices are retained for the legally required period for audit.
🗣️ In plain words: the machines don't report people's data; the only personal info is the business contacts, which we can delete on request except where law requires keeping invoices.
💬 One-liner: "Telemetry is PII-free; business contacts have retention + erasure, invoices kept for audit/legal."

🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is the back office for a vending-machine leasing business — it tracks the machines, who leases each one, watches their live health and stock, and bills customers automatically. The design splits into two halves. The read half is a telemetry pipeline: two hundred thousand machines phone home every minute through a gateway into Kafka, a stream processor dedups and fans the data into a Redis live-status cache the dashboard reads and a time-series database for history, and a sweeper watches for machines that go silent. The transaction half is a Postgres-backed control plane for machines, leases, and invoices, where the hardest problem is billing money exactly once — solved by billing off a finalized nightly usage rollup with invoices keyed uniquely by business-and-period so a retry can never double-charge. The two halves connect once a month when the billing service joins lease terms with the reconciled telemetry rollup. And the single principle every hard decision traces back to is: keep the high-volume eventual telemetry plane physically separate from the low-volume exact money plane, and only join them on settled, authoritative data."
